provenance_label: Open-source research (OSINT) — individually sourced and cross-checked, not official government data
source_name: Atlas of Surveillance (Electronic Frontier Foundation + Reynolds School of Journalism, UNR)
data_vintage: Atlas of Surveillance dataset last updated 2025-12-04

metrics.label: U.S. law enforcement agencies documented using Flock Safety ALPR
ALPR camera nodes tagged worldwide (OpenStreetMap, secondary/crowdsourced)

example_entries.detail: Began installing Flock Safety ALPRs in 2018, expanded in 2020.
Operates Flock Safety automated license plate readers.
Operates three Flock Safety ALPRs as of September 2025.
Announced installation of 10 Flock Safety ALPRs, January 2023.

note: The headline count (2,707 agencies) comes from EFF's Atlas of Surveillance, a research project where trained volunteers and journalism students document each installation individually with a citation (news article, public record, or government document), reviewed by multiple people before publication. That's a meaningfully higher bar than anonymous map tagging, but it's still not an official government census — it reflects what EFF's team has documented as of their last data pull (Dec 4, 2025), not real-time reality. The OpenStreetMap figure below it is a separate, purely crowdsourced worldwide count included for context, not U.S.-specific and not independently verified. Example entries below are illustrative, not the full dataset — see the source link for the searchable database of all 2,707 agencies.
